Transcrição Completa: 2 Organic Content Engine Best Practices - My Current Set Up 12 13 25
[00:00] All right, welcome into the weekly group call. So, this week specifically, there was a lot of you that you didn't necessarily even recognize that this was your problem, but it was clear as fuck day your problem. Your organic content engine sucks ass. Like, you've done a terrible job of being able to systematize making a lot of organic content. And I think that's a problem that a lot of people struggle with.
[00:30] Um, so what I'm going to do in this video in particular, I'm going to cover all of the organic content engine best practices that we've found dial in the efficiency of being able to churn out a ton of organic content on a week-week basis and how we also intend on amplifying it all in a really easy way.
[00:48] Let me start with this. Okay, the total amount of time that I put in per week is about 2 to three hours on content production. Okay. Can you go much harder than that? Yeah, absolutely. Do I have more time than that to invest into it right now? No.
[01:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "2-3 Hours Weekly Content Production"
NÚMEROS: 2-3 horas por semana
VISUAL: Conceito de eficiência temporal, comparação entre tempo investido vs resultado obtido, demonstração de sistema otimizado
[01:10] Uh, does it make me a lot of money? Yeah. Could I justify putting more time into it? Yeah. However, I don't. Okay. I don't because I feel like my current system can be amplified exponentially more than it is now. And I think you'll agree to the same by the end of this whole thing.
[01:30] The two to three hours weekly. This is the part that a lot of you seem to struggle with the most is just the lesson of efficiency in general. Like when you think of doing anything, you just have an unbound amount of time that you consider putting and as a result of putting an unbound amount of time towards it. You consistently feel extremely overwhelmed when you go to take on anything new.
[01:55] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Parkinson's Law"
NÚMEROS: 10 anos (previsão inicial) vs 8 meses (resultado real) vs 6 meses (meta estabelecida)
VISUAL: Conceito de lei de Parkinson aplicada à produção de conteúdo, comparação temporal de produtividade, exemplo do foguete Falcon 9 da SpaceX
[02:10] So, let me just be really clear and reiterate this simple lesson for you all, which is really important. You understand this Parkinson's law. We've talked about this a tremendous amount of times. It's the Elon Musk lesson where I taught you guys about the rocket team that he hired to build that one that lands itself, the giant one that's going to go to Mars. I think it's called like the Falcon 9 heavy or something like that.
[02:35] His team of scientists, mathematicians, physicists, engineers, they all told I'm going to mute you all so you guys shut up. Okay, excuse me, Omar. Okay. So anyway, long story short, the Parkinson's law concept came from Elon Musk when he had that team of rocket scientists, engineers, mathematicians, scientists. They told him mathematically it was going to take a decade to be able to build that rocket.
[03:05] There was a whole bunch of shit for it that didn't exist. Long story short, since I'm sure you've heard it from me before, moral of the story is he did it in 8 months because he pressed the fuck out of his team. He told him it was only going to take six months and therefore they managed to accomplish it in a much shorter time frame than what they had previously all mathematically calculated it to be possible to do.
[03:30] The main thing I want to make extremely clear is when I look at this is like a 2 to three hour a week task. I went into it with the concept of Parkinson's law in mind. Okay. So even if you at the end of all this believe deeply that it's going to take you more time than that. I just want you to remember you still want to have a very tight time constraint to it so you can operate within that time frame and accomplish it in a far shorter duration than what you might otherwise consider.
[04:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Sunday 1-4 PM EST Production Schedule"
NÚMEROS: 1:00 PM - 4:00 PM EST (horário fixo dominical)
VISUAL: Rotina semanal estruturada, bloco de tempo dedicado, consistência de agendamento
[04:05] Um, I do my 2 to 3 hours on Sunday. Okay? Usually from about 1 to 300 p.m. uh EST. I will be honest in saying it generally goes from about 1 to 4:00 p.m. EST. That's where the additional third hour comes in. Sometimes I sit down and I'm honestly super efficient. But I don't know how y'all operate in your creative process, but for me, I just need a little bit of time to kind of flow.
[04:35] Sometimes that could mean just listening to some music. Sometimes I'm very extroverted and I need to kind of like ideulate and think out loud, and other times I'm just already well equipped with whatever I need to sit down and actually shoot.

NÚMEROS: 3 vídeos por semana (1 vlog + 2 talking head)

[04:55] U let me be clear on this. This all goes towards long form content, okay? And specifically, it goes towards what we do on YouTube. Okay, my YouTube content is a combination right now of essentially three different pieces of content that go out per week.
[05:15] Okay, so the first thing happens on a Monday. It's our vlog. The vlog is thankfully just captured doing things that I'm already otherwise doing. So there's no real additional time involved with that. It's more so just documentation of something we're already doing.
[05:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Vlog Strategy - Documentation Over Creation"
NÚMEROS: Segunda-feira (vlog), Quarta-feira (talking head), Sábado (talking head)
VISUAL: Estratégia de documentação vs criação, conceito de "get to know me content", desenvolvimento de personagem através de vlogs
[05:40] The other two are talking head content. Talking head content is likely what a majority of you watched and saw in order to help support the sales process to buy into something like this. These three pieces of content, okay, the vlog, like I said, is just kind of shot throughout the week.
[06:00] One of the number one roles that you can hire in your organization is a videographer to literally just follow you around. They don't have to do it all the time. I don't like go out of my way and intentionally craft and curate vlogs. We just have shit going on all the time. So, it's super easy to capture.
[06:20] I've seen zero financial upside from my vlog so far. I'm not in any way, shape, or form trying to have some type of implication that would lead you to believe otherwise. The vlog is exclusively for what I call get to know me content.
[06:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Character Development Through Content"
NÚMEROS: Zero retorno financeiro direto (até o momento)
VISUAL: Conceito de desenvolvimento de personagem, criação de familiaridade, construção de confiança através de conteúdo variado
[06:45] I deeply believe in the concept of character development with organic content where rather than just having a one-sided person, in this case it would be all my talking head content that would give a very one-sided impression. I believe the vlog can add more character depth to who I am as a person in the eyes of people who watch and consume my info on the internet.
[07:10] I believe the vlog can help lead to additional trust. I believe the vlog can lead to additional familiarity bias. Um the great great effect of this is like people walk up to me and they already actively know what's going on in my life. They feel like they already know me.
[07:30] They just talk to me and engage as though they've had like a longtime relationship with me. And obviously I have no idea who they are in almost all instances, but you know that's the effective content.

[08:05] Um, a lot of people act like it's super fucking hard to do, and I think that's because you're being very performative.
[08:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Performative Male Concept"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Conceito de autenticidade vs performance, exemplo do homem lendo livro em café para impressionar, comparação entre ser genuíno e fingir
[08:20] It's a really good joke that currently is a meme inside of uh I don't know wherever the fuck you watch content. I've seen a lot on Instagram. They call it being a performative male. Anybody ever heard of this? Put a one in the chat if you heard of this? Put a two in the chat if you have no idea what the fuck I'm talking about.
[08:45] You heard of what a performative male is? Okay, so I see a bunch of twos. So here's how this joke kind of started. So, there was this guy. He's sitting at a cafe. He was reading a book from a well-known influencer lady. And he wasn't even really reading the book. He just kind of had it opened up. He was like looking at it passively and then he'd kind of like look up to see if any girls were looking at him.
[09:15] And then he would kind of like go back to like pretending to read the book. And the joke initially started with that specific video. It's called being a performative male. It's essentially a very simple concept to understand. The concept is you're pretending to be something and as a result of pretending to be it, when people watch you in that condition, they think that, oh, this guy's faking it, you know, like this doesn't look real.
[09:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Authenticity in Content Creation"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Diferença entre performance teatral e autenticidade, impacto na qualidade do conteúdo, redução de erros e bloopers
[09:50] And then it ended up spinning out into like an entire meme where specifically in New York, they found a lot of these performative males, but in much more like terrible conditions. Like these would be guys that walk around in a very specific type of outfit and you know they'd have like a little tote bag and then somebody would walk up and they'd be like, "Oh, what's in your tote bag?"
[10:15] And the person would pull out like a dictionary or some shit like that, you know, and they'd be like, "What kind of music are you listening to?" They they would say like a very uh like if you said that you were listening to it, you know, you'd probably be listening to it exclusively because of what other people may think of you if they caught you listening to it kind of situation.
[10:40] So anyway, when I say you want to avoid at all costs being what's called a performative male, the concept is really easy to understand and practice when you sit down to do a YouTube video. Like I don't pretend to be anything I'm not. Okay? I just sit down. I'm my truest self and I just talk like I'm talking to a person.

Uh, I give this lesson a lot inside of sales where I'm like, you ever hear a salesperson that all of a sudden just has a completely different voice when they talk to somebody on the phone and then they hang up and they talk to you and you're like, dude, why the fuck are you talking like that when you're on a sales call?
[11:40] It's cuz they they act like in the sales process that they're required to speak like a completely different way. It's not true, okay? It's not true and it's not how it is. you just want to sit down and you want to truly be yourself. And that's going to be the easiest way to not fuck up and create like an ungodly amount of content just from how many bloopers you're going to end up having.
[12:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Content Ideation Process - Real-Time Observation"
NÚMEROS: Ideias acumuladas ao longo da semana inteira
VISUAL: Processo de coleta de ideias através de interações com clientes, observação de padrões, identificação de problemas recorrentes
[12:10] Um, the ideiation process is also something that people really struggle with when it comes to content. I actively pull all of my content ideas throughout my normal work week. They all scream at me throughout the out the week for exactly what I should talk about next. How do I do that? Really fucking easy. I talk to you guys all the time. I talk to my clients all the time.
[12:35] I'm actively engaged inside of all my group chats where all my customers and clients sit there and talk to each other and I can literally watch from above. If you were like an air traffic control employee at an airport, right, and you sit in that tower all day, you see a completely different perspective of the airport than the people who are inside of the planes landing, right?
[13:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Air Traffic Control Analogy"
NÚMEROS: Dezenas de aviões por hora vs 1 voo por ano (perspectiva individual)
VISUAL: Analogia de controle de tráfego aéreo, visão panorâmica vs visão individual, acúmulo de experiência através de observação
[13:05] It's like, think about, do you guys even know how many planes land within an hour, right? It's like somebody who's afraid of flying, that might be their only flight within like a year, right? Do you think the air traffic control people are afraid of flying when they watch dozens of planes land and take off per hour? Some specific airports have an even higher quantity of traffic than that.
[13:30] The point I'm attempting to make is when you look at your content ideas, they should all be so fucking obvious to you and and you should be able to sit down and just reflect within like a couple minutes and be like, "That's what I'm going to talk about this week."
[13:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "One Person Says It = 1000 People Think It"
NÚMEROS: 1 pessoa menciona = 1.000 pessoas pensando o mesmo
VISUAL: Regra de multiplicação de problemas, princípio de representatividade de feedback, extrapolação de necessidades
[13:50] Let me give you a great example of this, okay? We used to go by this rule when I worked at Grant's office back in the day. We used to say, "If one person says it, there's a thousand people thinking it that haven't said it yet." Okay? So sometimes the power is is just like in one conversation that one of you guys intimately shares with me or in like one message I see in the group chat or like in one specific client win or loss that occurs.
[14:20] I'm like I bet there's a fuckload of people dealing with that and then I sit down and I make a video on it and I'm right every time because I just go by that rule. If one person says it or one person experiences it or if you can observe it in one person, there's likely a much higher multiple of people that are dealing with it that just aren't actively bringing it to your awareness.
[14:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Real-Time Ideation vs Pre-Planning"
NÚMEROS: Tempo de ideação durante filmagem vs ao longo da semana

[14:50] Okay. So, the other issue with ideiation is is you guys look for things that are like substantial in in feedback loops, right? You'd act like you need a whole bunch of people to tell you things before you sit down and make a video. I think that's the wrong way to go about it. the ideation process. When I say it should be something that's done in real time, I I mean it in every way, shape, or form.
[15:15] I think that you should just go in that time slot. As an example, let's say that we're sitting down for 1 to 3 p.m. and we're going to shoot content. It's like throughout the week, I open up my notes folder on Telegram and I just type in random shit that comes up throughout the week and I'm like, bang, that's a great idea.

So, it's like some of you guys, you tell me you struggle with attracting the wrong types of people consistently and then when I look at your stuff, it's glaringly obvious to me as to why you're attracting skeptics.

I always tell you guys, anytime you make some kind of income claim, you immediately are attracting a much lower qualified demographic than what you'd think. Anytime that you aren't actively discussing risks associated with your income claims, you're just further burying yourself and poor people that are going to be responsive to your ads and your messaging, right?
[16:30] So, it's like I saw that in a few of you this week, took note of it and was like, bang, we're going to make a nice piece of content on that. It's like my point being the ideation process should not happen when you go to shoot. [17:20] If you guys are attempting to post content in an effort to attract your ideal client and customer, literally all you got to do is just go talk to your current clients and customers and look at their problems that you're helping them solve and then make content on that. It's not harder than that. It's literally that easy.

[18:05] Okay, so for me, I have a few environments that I'll consistently shoot. I got the penthouse, I got the office, and I have the jungle house. Okay, these are three environments in which I can consistently shoot. Okay, and in those environments, it's like I also have a few key things.

In my office, it's the same thing. It's like I got a few places I can shoot. I got the big screen and I got this like chair that we put in front of the big screen recently. I've got the conference table.
[18:50] And then in addition to that, we kind of leave some room for randomness and we try some different some different place like the ping pong table, the pool table. We shot a video on the fucking golf cart once. Like you know, you can have some randomness factored into in the jungle house. There's really only one place that we shoot and that's the backyard. Backyard's the best place. It's by the bamboo if you've ever seen a video back there.

What you have to have here, and the reason I bring this up, it might seem like such a simple thing, but it's not. It's like when I'm like, "All right, I need to go film videos." All I got to do immediately is just, "All right, where do we want to film?"
[19:50] And I got to have all the environments in my head already ready to go because that's going to determine a lot. That's going to determine if I need to go here to grab that and then go back there to fucking shoot.

[27:05] Okay, the equipment is something to consider. And then in addition to format, it's like you have to also consider style. Okay, so as you guys all know, I'm sure like my specific style is is what I call visual. Okay, so my visual style is uh drawing on an iPad. Pretty cool, huh? Look, we're doing it right now.
[27:30] So, moral of the story, this is my This is how I do it. Like, I film myself, I screen record the iPad, I send those off to my editing team, and then they do everything from there. Um, I picked visual for my specific style because that's how I communicate. Okay?
[27:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Three Learning/Teaching Styles"
NÚMEROS: 3 estilos principais (Auditivo, Visual, Cinestésico)
VISUAL: Tipos de comunicação e aprendizado, preferências de output, alcance demográfico por estilo
[27:55] Everybody has a preferred output and input for their learning mechanisms and how they teach. Uh some people are auditory, meaning some people can literally just explain stuff and that's how that's how their preferred output is. U some people like me were more visual and then there's also what's called kinesthetic. Kinesesthetic is like emotion or giving examples or like literally showing people how to do stuff um through exercises and things like that.
[28:30] The moral of the story is I found visual to be the best for me because again that's just my preferred output and a majority of people on earth are also visual. So I knew I'd also appeal to the most people.

[32:40] So, a tryhard is a very simple thing to understand. Okay. It's exactly as described. It's somebody who tries way too fucking hard and therefore they don't have fun. They burn themselves out and they rapidly deteriorate in their mental state and they get really mad. They're really easy to rage bait. They're simply put people who just sit down and try to crank the effort bar to like the fullest possible extent. And as a result of doing so, they burn themselves out extremely quickly.
[33:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Car Engine Analogy - Redlining"

[33:20] In the in the comparison of a car, as an example, if I just literally pressed the gas pedal all the way to the floor and that's how I constantly drove around and I just redlined my vehicle all the time, I'm going to fuck up the engine of my car. like the car quality and the and the ability of the car is going to deteriorate dramatically faster than if I drove the car like somewhat regularly while occasionally kicking it up faster.
[33:50] You know, you just can't make content sitting down and being a tryhard every time. Extroversion means you say shit out loud to process thoughts. Okay, I'm very extroverted. VISUAL: Exemplos de manipulação de plataforma, banners de scam pagos, vulnerabilidades de segurança
[55:00] I hit up Jeremy Moser, the organic Instagram OG here in the group. And this motherfucker tells me I'm cooked. And to just Oh, you got to create another profile now, Jeremy. Yeah, right, dude. I'm not fucking doing that. Get fucked
[55:20] I I'm I'm again I'm not building a lot of revenue on Instagram because it's one of the most unstable, inconsistent, completely manipulated places you could build an organic business. I I do not feel safe in any way, shape, or form building a business organically on Instagram.
[55:40] If my revenue actively grows from posting on Instagram, I get more anxious as a result of thinking now people who I don't fucking know, who just don't like me, have more leverage over me and can just pay an Indian 500 bucks or a,000 bucks to take me down.

[1:23:20] Within the first couple weeks, like within that month of July, I think we saw a very small uptick in education company buyers. And I remember thinking, well, this isn't what I wanted. You know, I I was again trying to get agency clients, but you know, it's it's revenue. We'll take it.
[1:23:40] The first month I paid 3K. I think the first month I made back at least like four or 5K. So, it was a good it was a good like initial test that proved out right away. And then every month after that, I've just added more and more and more and more revenue. Like the growth has continued to compound as a result of the channel's growth continuing to compound. So, it was pretty quick in terms of how fast I saw the payoff.

[1:27:10] That's when you justify test dollars going towards tests is when you're your most profitable. That's when it's easiest to test. That's when it's pressure-free. Okay? [1:27:35] I think a 3k cost per month or like 4K a month, whatever the editors are going to charge you nowadays, I don't think that's that that's tough to justify.

[1:49:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Two Phases of Organic Content"

[1:50:00] That is an unknown, rather uncontrolled and unpredictable thing to occur. You don't know when the tipping point is and it could be at any point. All I can tell you is still worth doing because you're going to see a lot more deals come through when you just support your paid sales process through making organic content and have a presence on different platforms where people are going to look you up anyway.
[1:50:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Message for Beginners - Sales Support First"

[1:50:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Message for Advanced - Amplification"

[1:50:50] And then obviously there's plenty of you here that have a fuckload going on organically. I just ask you what your amplifiers are going to be. What are you going to do? It's like what we talked about last week in relation to goal setting and grand vision.
[1:51:10] When you break down 2026 and you look at the successful actions that you want to amplify, you want to find ways to be able to go well above and beyond what you're currently doing and get additional eyeballs to it. If you already act as a discovery mechanism, what's going to be the changes that amplify all of it for you? That'd be the question I ask you.
[1:51:40] How could you add or evolve your current system would be the other question I'd ask you.
[1:51:50] All right, very good. Great call everybody. Honor to help you per usual. Follow up with me if y'all got questions afterwards. Let's go get some fucking money, huh? Talk soon.
📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
INVESTIMENTO E CUSTOS:
2-3 horas semanais de produção de conteúdo pessoal
Domingo 1-4 PM EST - horário fixo de filmagem
3 vídeos por semana: 1 vlog (segunda), 2 talking head (quarta/sábado)
Todos publicados às 12:00 EST
CUSTOS MENSAIS:
YouTube Editing: $6K/mês (inclui vlogs)
Instagram (Phillip): $4K/mês
X/Twitter + LinkedIn (Marcos/Bird House): $6.3K/mês
TOTAL: $16.3K/mês
EQUIPAMENTO INICIAL:
2 iPads
1 tripé para iPad
Microfones Road + cabo especial
Duração: 8-9 meses com este setup
EQUIPAMENTO ATUAL:
2 câmeras DSLR
Microfones (Road/Lavalier)
2 tripés
2+ painéis LED de iluminação
Tela de $40.000 (220 polegadas)
RESULTADOS:
Julho 2024: Início do teste
Primeiro mês: Investiu $3K, retornou $4-5K
~5.000 subs ao começar
Crescimento composto mensal desde então
8 meses manteve $3K/mês antes de expandir
Zero retorno financeiro de vlogs (até agora)
WARDROBE:
90 camisetas pretas idênticas
12 calças Palm Angel
2 pares de sapatos
Poucos relógios e pulseiras
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
SISTEMA DE PRODUÇÃO:
Ideação contínua: Coleta de ideias ao longo da semana via interações com clientes
Regra 1:1000: Se 1 pessoa menciona = 1.000 pensam o mesmo
Notas no Telegram: Captura rápida de ideias durante a semana
Filmagem em lote: 2 vídeos por sessão dominical
Duração dos vídeos: 15 minutos a 1 hora cada
PROCESSO DE FILMAGEM:
Almoço (energia)
Música (flow state)
Ideação verbal (extrovertido)
Filmagem imediata após ideação completa
Pausa curta
Repetir para segundo vídeo
Upload para Google Drive
Handoff total para equipe
FORMATO (4 COMPONENTES):
Ambiente: 3 locações principais (Penthouse, Office, Jungle House)
Equipamento: iPad ou DSLR (resultado igual)
Estilo: Visual (desenho no iPad + screen recording)
Rotina: Domingo 1-4 PM EST (inquebrável)
AMPLIFICAÇÃO DE CONTEÚDO:
Hub: Google Drive com footage
Instagram: 1-2 shorts diários (Phillip) → aprovação via Telegram → postagem imediata
X/Twitter: Todos tweets derivados de YouTube → push back para YouTube
LinkedIn: Newsletters + posts derivados de YouTube → push back para YouTube
YouTube Shorts: NÃO usado ("poor people platform")
TikTok: NÃO usado atualmente
DISCOVERY HIERARCHY:
#1 Word of Mouth (maioria dos compradores)
#2 Outras plataformas sociais → YouTube
#3 YouTube direto
TRACKING:
URLs específicas por plataforma
Formulários únicos por canal
Sem Hyros (custo evitado)
ESTRUTURA:
TIPOS DE CONTEÚDO:
Vlog (Segunda): Documentação, não criação - "get to know me content"
Talking Head (Quarta): Conteúdo educacional técnico
Talking Head (Sábado): Conteúdo educacional técnico
FILOSOFIAS FUNDAMENTAIS:
Lei de Parkinson:
Foguete Falcon 9: 10 anos previstos → 6 meses meta → 8 meses real
Constraint de tempo força eficiência
Autenticidade:
"Performative Male" - evitar a todo custo
Ser verdadeiro self, não teatro
Mesma voz em vendas e conteúdo
Decisão-Making Fatigue:
Eliminar escolhas desnecessárias
Mesmo ambiente/roupa = conservação de energia mental
Foco em produção, não em logística
Rotina Inquebrável:
Exemplo: Recusar festa do pai da namorada
Exemplo: Filmaria durante furacão à luz de velas
Nada quebra o padrão domingo 1-4 PM EST
Equipment Philosophy:
"Equipment doesn't make a lick of a fucking difference"
iPad = DSLR em termos de resultado
Execução > Perfeição técnica
Agência vs In-House:
Agências têm experiência exponencial acumulada
Exposure a múltiplos casos vs 1 negócio
Expertise especializada vs conhecimento generalista
Conteúdo como Suporte:
Fase 1 (início): Suporte a processo de vendas pago
Fase 2 (crescimento): Mecanismo de descoberta
Tipping point imprevisível mas inevitável
Plataformas:
YouTube: Confiável, long-form, compradores sérios
Instagram: Instável, manipulável, baixo investimento estratégico
LinkedIn: "Spam hellhole" pessoalmente, mas delega conteúdo
X/Twitter: Crescimento após Marcos
Demographic Awareness:
Clippers atraem "retards" e "donkeys"
Curadoria cuidadosa necessária
Vende apenas para "rich people getting richer"
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
ROI TIMELINE:
Mês 1 (Julho 2024): -$3K + $4-5K = +$1-2K lucro
Meses 2-8: Crescimento composto contínuo
Mês 9+: Expansão para $16.3K/mês investimento
Justificativa: Já fazia >$16.3K/mês em receita antes de expandir
INVESTIMENTO PROGRESSIVO:
Fase 1 (8 meses): $3K/mês (YouTube apenas)
Fase 2 (atual): $16.3K/mês (YouTube + Instagram + X/LinkedIn)
Vlogs: $6K/mês adicional (sem ROI ainda, mas "bet")
PRODUTOS VENDIDOS:
Done-For-You (DFY): Serviços de agência
Done-With-You (DWY): Inner Circle + MIMP
MIMP: $5K (sem financiamento)
CLIENT BREAKDOWN:
Maioria: Clientes educação (Inner Circle/MIMP)
Minoria: Clientes agência (padrões extremamente altos)
100% compradores high-ticket: Assistem long-form
Zero compradores high-ticket: De shorts/reels/TikTok
FUNDING PHILOSOPHY:
Sem financiamento em MIMP
Não colocar pessoas em dívida
Requisito de capacidade financeira real ($5K)
COMPARAÇÕES:
Brian Mark: $1M+/mês apenas Instagram DMs
Brez: $1M/mês vlog → talking head (sistema inverso)
Jeremy Moser: Especialista Instagram, múltiplos $1M/mês clients
DURAÇÃO: ~1h52min FORMATO: Group call educacional, demonstração ao vivo no iPad, Q&A interativo
